q_striae_style.question: What is the style of the underside postdiscal striae (the transverse markings)?
q_striae_style.hint: The bochus group has characteristic lunulate, buff-tipped (scalloped, comma-like) striae — pale buff marks with a rounded tip, giving a scalloped appearance. All other Malaysian Jamides have white, straight or slightly wavy striae. This is usually the easiest first separation in the genus.

choices.label: Lunulate, buff-tipped (scalloped or comma-like striae)
White, straight or slightly wavy striae
Cannot determine striae style

q_inverted.question: Is the underside ground colour white with blackish-brown markings (an inverted pattern unusual for the genus)?
q_inverted.hint: In Jamides yehi, the ground colour is white and the markings are blackish-brown — the reverse of the typical Jamides pattern (pale ground with white striae). Despite the unusual pattern, J. yehi retains the characteristic black tornal spot crowned with orange on the HW. This species is exceptionally rare and known from only a single specimen from Tapah Hills, Perak. In a normal specimen the ground is grey or pale grey and the striae are white.

choices.label: Yes — white ground colour with blackish-brown markings (inverted pattern)
No — grey or pale grey ground with white striae
Cannot determine

q_hw_wedge_spots.question: On the hindwing underside, are the submarginal spots prominently black and wedge-shaped (∧), elongated spots between the inner and outer submarginal striae?
q_hw_wedge_spots.hint: In J. aratus adana and J. philatus subditus, the spaces between the inner and outer submarginal striae on the HW underside contain prominent black wedge-shaped (∧) spots. This pattern is shared by both species and is not found in other Malaysian Jamides. The two are easily separated by their very different upperside colour — J. aratus is almost white on both surfaces; J. philatus male is sky blue.

choices.label: Yes — prominent black wedge-shaped (∧) spots on HW submarginal area
No — submarginal spots absent or not wedge-shaped (∧)

q_aratus_philatus_location.question: Where was this individual photographed?
q_aratus_philatus_location.hint: Both J. aratus adana and J. philatus subditus have identical HW underside patterns, but their distributions barely overlap. J. aratus adana is common on Pulau Tioman and other east-coast islands of Peninsular Malaysia and has never been recorded on the mainland or Borneo. J. philatus subditus is an uncommon forest species of mainland Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, and Sabah (Borneo). If the upperside is visible: J. aratus is almost white on both surfaces (male has a diffuse ~1.0mm black FW border and a double series of dark HW submarginal spots); J. philatus male is sky blue with a thread-like FW border and no HW submarginal spots, female pale blue.

choices.label: Pulau Tioman or another east-coast island of Peninsular Malaysia
Mainland Peninsular Malaysia (forest), Singapore, or Sabah (Borneo)
Cannot determine / location unknown

q_hw_sub_dark.question: Are the spaces between the HW inner and outer submarginal striae distinctly darkened?
q_hw_sub_dark.hint: Jamides cyta minna is uniquely distinguished by having the spaces between the inner and outer submarginal striae on the HW underside strongly filled with dark brown — forming a conspicuous dark band. J. cyta is also one of the largest Malaysian Jamides. Jamides lugine (Bornean endemic) has these spaces only barely darker than the ground colour, and its overall underside colour and pattern are duller and more greyish-brown — approaching J. bochus — despite having straight white striae, unlike the pale grey ground typical of other celeno-group/cyta-subgroup species. In all other species the submarginal area is not distinctly darkened.

choices.label: Yes — strongly filled with dark brown (conspicuous dark band)
Barely darker than ground, with the overall underside duller and more greyish-brown (approaching J. bochus) despite straight white striae — Bornean species
No — submarginal area not distinctly darkened
Cannot determine

q_fw_vein3.question: Is the forewing postdiscal band completely dislocated at vein 3 — the portion in space 3 shifted basad (inward), out of line with the band in spaces 4 and 5?
q_fw_vein3.hint: This separates the two main remaining groups of Malaysian Jamides. In the elpis subgroup (J. elpis, J. caeruleus, J. alecto, J. abdul, J. cunilda, J. limes, J. talinga, J. ferrari), the FW postdiscal band is completely dislocated at vein 3 — the portion in space 3 is clearly shifted inward out of line with spaces 4 and 5. In the celeno subgroup (J. celeno, J. pura, J. zebra, J. malaccanus, J. parasaturatus), the band runs continuously from vein 3 to vein 7, with space 3 in line with spaces 4 and 5.

choices.label: Yes — band completely dislocated at vein 3 (space 3 portion shifted inward)
No — band continuous, space 3 more or less in line with spaces 4 and 5
Cannot determine dislocation at vein 3

q_fw_vein6.question: Is the forewing postdiscal band also slightly dislocated at vein 6 — the portion in space 6 shifted basad (inward)?
q_fw_vein6.hint: In Jamides abdul, the FW band is dislocated ONLY at vein 3; the portion in space 6 is only slightly curved, not shifted inward. In all other elpis-subgroup members (J. elpis, J. caeruleus, J. alecto, J. cunilda, J. limes, J. talinga, J. ferrari), the band is also slightly dislocated at vein 6, with the portion in space 6 shifted basad. J. abdul has a highly distinctive coppery green male upperside with very broad diffuse black borders.

choices.label: Yes — band also slightly dislocated at vein 6 (space 6 shifted inward)
No — band dislocated at vein 3 only; space 6 not shifted inward
Cannot determine dislocation at vein 6

q_elpis_costal.question: On the forewing underside, are there distinct white costal dashes above the end-cell bar?
q_elpis_costal.hint: Three elpis-subgroup species have a pair of distinct white costal dashes on the FW underside above the end-cell bar: J. cunilda nisanca, J. limes, and J. talinga talinga. The remaining four species (J. elpis, J. alecto, J. caeruleus, J. ferrari) typically lack costal dashes.

choices.label: Yes — distinct white costal dashes above the FW end-cell bar
No — no costal dashes above end-cell bar
Cannot determine

q_elpis_talinga.question: On the hindwing underside, are the subbasal striae dislocated (the paired lines broken or offset, not running as a continuous unbroken pair)?
q_elpis_talinga.hint: In Jamides talinga talinga, the smallest elpis-subgroup species (FW <15mm), the HW subbasal striae are characteristically dislocated rather than continuous — a diagnostic character within the group. J. talinga also has the broadest FW border of the no-vein-6-skip species (1.0mm at tornus, ≥2.0mm at apex). Both J. cunilda and J. limes have continuous (unbroken) HW subbasal striae.

choices.label: Yes — HW subbasal striae dislocated (not running as a continuous pair)
No — HW subbasal striae continuous (unbroken paired lines)
Cannot determine

q_elpis_striae_costal.question: Are the white underside striae broad and rather diffuse, or narrow and clearly defined?
q_elpis_striae_costal.hint: This separates J. limes (Bornean; broad and diffuse striae; large, FW ≥18mm) from J. cunilda nisanca (narrow and clearly defined striae; mainland Malay Peninsula and nearby islands). Both have FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6, costal dashes, and a large orange tornal area on the HW. In J. cunilda the large tornal area often obliterates the outer postdiscal stria in HW space 2 (sometimes also space 3).

choices.label: Broad and rather diffuse (larger species, FW ≥18mm; Bornean)
Narrow and clearly defined
Cannot determine

q_elpis_striae_nocostal.question: Are the white underside striae slightly broader and more diffuse than typical for the genus?
q_elpis_striae_nocostal.hint: In Jamides alecto ageladas (the largest Malayan elpis-subgroup species, FW >15mm), the white striae are slightly broader and more diffuse than in the other members without costal dashes. J. elpis, J. caeruleus, and J. ferrari all have narrow, clearly defined striae. J. alecto is also the only species in this group without costal dashes to have black marginal spots on the HW upperside.

choices.label: Slightly broader and more diffuse (larger species, FW >15mm)
Narrow and clearly defined
Cannot determine

q_elpis_hw_stria7.question: On the HW underside, where is the second white stria from the base in space 7 positioned relative to the cell striae?
q_elpis_hw_stria7.hint: Look at the second stria from the base in HW space 7 and compare its position to the two nearest striae in the cell. In J. elpis pseudelpis, this stria sits about midway between the 2nd and 3rd cell striae. In J. caeruleus caeruleus and J. ferrari evansi, it is in line with or closer to the 3rd cell stria (the outer of the relevant pair). This character separates elpis from the caeruleus/ferrari pair.

choices.label: About midway between the 2nd and 3rd cell striae (middle position)
In line with or closer to the 3rd cell stria (outer position)
Cannot determine HW stria 7 position

q_ferrari_check.question: In the female: is the lowermost HW submarginal spot on the underside distinctly elongated or linear (rather than rounded)?
q_ferrari_check.hint: The female of J. ferrari evansi has the lowermost HW submarginal spot in the tornal area characteristically linear (elongated). In J. caeruleus caeruleus the corresponding spot is rounded. For male specimens: J. ferrari male has battledore (paddle-shaped) androconia, is paler blue with distinct HW submarginal markings; J. caeruleus male lacks battledore androconia and is a brilliant metallic caerulean blue. J. ferrari is much rarer than J. caeruleus.

choices.label: Female: lowermost HW submarginal spot distinctly linear (elongated)
Female: lowermost HW submarginal spot rounded
Cannot determine (male specimen, or spot shape unclear)

q_celeno_hw_pairs.question: On the hindwing underside, how many pairs of continuous white lines are present?
q_celeno_hw_pairs.hint: In the celeno subgroup, J. malaccanus malaccanus and J. parasaturatus paramalaccanus are distinguished from J. celeno, J. pura, and J. zebra by having two pairs of continuous white lines on the HW underside: a subbasal pair and a discal pair. The three celeno/pura/zebra species each have only a single (subbasal) pair of HW white lines.

choices.label: Two pairs of continuous white lines (subbasal pair and discal pair)
One pair of white lines (subbasal pair only)
Cannot determine number of HW line pairs

q_celeno_transparency_two.question: Are the underside white markings clearly visible from the upperside by transparency? (Jamides malaccanus vs parasaturatus — upperside or back-lit character)
q_celeno_transparency_two.hint: Both J. malaccanus malaccanus and J. parasaturatus paramalaccanus have two pairs of HW white lines. The key difference: in J. parasaturatus, the underside markings are clearly visible through the wings from the upperside; in J. malaccanus, they are barely visible. Additional upperside differences: J. malaccanus male is deeper sky blue with distinct black submarginal spots in the tornal half of the HW (and has battledore androconia); J. parasaturatus male is paler blue without HW submarginal markings.

choices.label: Yes — underside markings clearly visible from upperside by transparency
No — underside markings barely or not visible from upperside
Cannot determine upperside transparency

q_celeno_fw_border.question: In the male, how broad is the forewing black border at the apex? (Jamides celeno vs pura/zebra — upperside character)
q_celeno_fw_border.hint: Jamides celeno aelianus has a conspicuously broad black FW border reaching at least 1.0mm at the apex. J. pura pura has a thread-like FW border. J. zebra lakatti has a very slightly and diffusely expanding border toward the apex — both pura and zebra borders are much narrower than celeno's. The female of J. celeno is also characteristically dingy whitish-blue, contrasting with the brighter, clearer females of J. pura and J. zebra.

choices.label: Broad, conspicuously wide — at least 1.0mm at apex (upperside)
Thread-like or very slightly expanding — much narrower than 1.0mm (upperside)
Cannot determine FW border width (upperside)

q_celeno_transparency_one.question: Are the underside markings clearly visible from the upperside by transparency? (Jamides pura vs zebra — upperside or back-lit character)
q_celeno_transparency_one.hint: Between J. pura pura and J. zebra lakatti (both with a single HW stria pair and a narrow FW border), the key difference is transparency. In J. pura, the underside markings are often clearly visible when viewed from the upperside. In J. zebra, the markings are barely visible. The male of J. zebra also has the blue deepening toward the FW apex with a slightly diffuse border expansion there — contrasting with J. pura's uniformly clear blue and thread-like border.

choices.label: Yes — underside markings clearly visible from upperside by transparency
No — underside markings barely visible from upperside
Cannot determine upperside transparency

r_bochus.name: Jamides bochus nabonassar
r_bochus.note: Bochus group (sole Malaysian representative). Distinguished from all other Jamides by its lunulate, pale buff (comma-like) postdiscal striae — all other species have white straight striae. Underside ground colour greyish-brown to ochreous brown. FW postdiscal band in space 3 in line with spaces 4–5. Male: brilliant dark metallic morpho-blue with very broad black FW border entering the upper cell; lacks androconia. Tailed at vein 2.

r_yehi.name: Jamides yehi
r_yehi.note: Celeno group, celeno subgroup. Exceptionally rare (known from a single specimen from Tapah Hills, Perak). White underside ground colour with blackish-brown markings — an inverted pattern unique in the genus. HW tornal area retains the characteristic black spot crowned with orange. FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Male lacks battledore androconia. FW ~19.0mm. Tailed at vein 2.

r_aratus.name: Jamides aratus adana
r_aratus.note: Celeno group, celeno subgroup. Common on Pulau Tioman and other east-coast islands of Peninsular Malaysia; not recorded on the Malay Peninsula mainland or in Borneo. Distinguished from J. philatus subditus by its almost white upperside (both sexes). HW underside: prominent black wedge-shaped (∧) submarginal spots. FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Male FW border diffuse, ~1.0mm; HW upperside with double series of dark submarginal spots. Tailed at vein 2.

r_philatus.name: Jamides philatus subditus
r_philatus.note: Celeno group, celeno subgroup. Uncommon; forest habitats of the Malay Peninsula, Singapore, and Sabah (Borneo). Distinguished from J. aratus adana by its blue upperside. HW underside: prominent black wedge-shaped (∧) submarginal spots (shared with J. aratus). FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Male: sky blue upperside with thread-like FW border; HW upperside lacks submarginal spots. Female: pale blue. Tailed at vein 2.

r_cyta.name: Jamides cyta minna
r_cyta.note: Cyta subgroup (sole Peninsular Malaysian member of the subgroup). Uniquely distinguished within the genus by the HW underside spaces between the inner and outer submarginal striae being strongly filled with dark brown. One of the largest Jamides. Male has distinctive long paddle-shaped androconia (not battledore) — unique in the genus. Male and female both blue upperside. Tailed at vein 2.

r_lugine.name: Jamides lugine
r_lugine.note: Cyta subgroup; Bornean endemic. The HW underside submarginal spaces are only barely darker than the ground colour (distinguishing it from J. cyta minna, where they are strongly filled with dark brown). The underside pattern and overall colour approach that of J. bochus. FW postdiscal band in space 3 in line with spaces 4–5 (band not dislocated at vein 3). Male: dull purple upperside with narrow black FW border; lacks androconia. FW 14–17mm. Tailed at vein 2.

r_abdul.name: Jamides abdul abdul
r_abdul.note: Elpis subgroup. Forest butterfly of the Malay Peninsula, also recorded in Singapore. FW band completely dislocated at vein 3 (elpis subgroup) but NOT dislocated at vein 6 — the portion in space 6 is only slightly curved. All white markings clearly defined. Male: distinctive coppery green upperside with very broad diffuse black borders; lacks androconia. Female: blue with broad black FW border extending narrowly along the costa to the wing base. Tailed at vein 2.

r_talinga.name: Jamides talinga talinga
r_talinga.note: Elpis subgroup. Smallest Malayan species in the elpis subgroup (FW <15mm). FW band dislocated at vein 3 and also slightly at vein 6. Distinct costal white dashes on FW above end-cell bar. The HW subbasal striae are dislocated (not running as a continuous pair) — diagnostic in the group. Also found in Kinabalu Park, Sabah. Male: shining blue upperside with conspicuously broad black FW border (1.0mm at tornus, increasing to at least 2.0mm at apex); lacks androconia. Female: pale silky blue. Tailed at vein 2.

r_limes.name: Jamides limes
r_limes.note: Elpis subgroup; Bornean species. FW band dislocated at vein 3 and also slightly at vein 6. Distinct costal white dashes on FW above end-cell bar. HW subbasal striae continuous. Striae broad and rather diffuse (vs narrow and clearly defined in J. cunilda). Large orange tornal area on HW. Large species (FW at least 18mm). Male: shining blue upperside with thin black border; lacks androconia. Tailed at vein 2.

r_cunilda.name: Jamides cunilda nisanca
r_cunilda.note: Elpis subgroup (= J. virgulatus in some older literature). FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6. Distinct costal white dashes on FW above end-cell bar. HW subbasal striae continuous. Striae narrow and clearly defined. Large orange tornal area on HW, often obliterating the outer postdiscal stria in HW space 2 (and sometimes space 3). Male: purple-blue upperside with usually thread-like (occasionally slightly expanding) FW border; lacks androconia. Female: much paler chalky blue; black FW border expands abruptly just above vein 4. Tailed at vein 2.

r_alecto.name: Jamides alecto ageladas
r_alecto.note: Elpis subgroup. The largest Malayan elpis-subgroup species (FW typically >15mm). FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6. No costal dashes on FW. Striae slightly broader and more diffuse than in the other no-costal-dashes members of the subgroup. Male: blue upperside with diffuse black FW border expanding to approximately 1.0mm at apex; black marginal spots on HW; lacks androconia. Female: blue with black FW border extending narrowly along the costa to the wing base. Tailed at vein 2.

r_elpis.name: Jamides elpis pseudelpis
r_elpis.note: Elpis subgroup. FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6. No costal dashes on FW. Striae narrow and clearly defined. The 2nd HW stria in space 7 is about midway between the 2nd and 3rd cell striae. Tornal area on HW not unusually large, not reaching the postdiscal stria in HW space 2. Small (FW <18mm). Male: pale shining sky-blue with thread-like FW border; underside markings visible from upperside by transparency. Female: pale blue with prominent black HW tornal spot, sometimes faintly orange-crowned. Tailed at vein 2.

r_ferrari.name: Jamides ferrari evansi
r_ferrari.note: Elpis subgroup. Rare forest species of the Malay Peninsula. FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6. No costal dashes on FW. Striae narrow and neat. The 2nd HW stria in space 7 is in line with or closer to the 3rd cell stria. Tornal area small, not reaching the postdiscal stria in HW space 2. Female: lowermost HW submarginal spot characteristically linear (elongated) — key underside diagnostic vs J. caeruleus. Male: paler blue than J. caeruleus; battledore androconia present; FW border expanding to approximately 1.0mm at apex; distinct HW submarginal markings. FW typically >15mm. Tailed at vein 2.

r_caeruleus.name: Jamides caeruleus caeruleus
r_caeruleus.note: Elpis subgroup. Common and widespread. FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6. No costal dashes on FW. Striae narrow and clearly defined. The 2nd HW stria in space 7 is in line with or closer to the 3rd cell stria (outer position). Tornal area normal to small. Female: lowermost HW submarginal spot rounded (cf. J. ferrari's linear spot). Male: brilliant metallic caerulean blue; FW border expanding to approximately 1.0mm at apex; lacks battledore androconia. Female: deeper and more shining blue than other elpis-subgroup species. FW typically >15mm. Tailed at vein 2.

r_malaccanus.name: Jamides malaccanus malaccanus
r_malaccanus.note: Celeno subgroup. Primary forest habitats; recently confirmed as resident in Singapore. HW underside with two pairs of continuous white lines (subbasal pair and discal pair). FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Underside markings barely visible from the upperside by transparency (vs clearly visible in J. parasaturatus). Male: deep sky blue upperside; distinct black submarginal spots in the tornal half of the HW upperside; battledore androconia present. Female: blue with veins sometimes slightly dark-dusted distally. Tailed at vein 2.

r_parasaturatus.name: Jamides parasaturatus paramalaccanus
r_parasaturatus.note: Celeno subgroup. Primary forest habitats. HW underside with two pairs of continuous white lines (subbasal pair and discal pair). FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Underside markings clearly visible from the upperside by transparency (vs barely visible in J. malaccanus). Male: paler blue than J. malaccanus; HW upperside lacks submarginal markings (present in malaccanus). Female: paler and clearer blue than J. malaccanus female. Tailed at vein 2.

r_celeno.name: Jamides celeno aelianus
r_celeno.note: Celeno subgroup. The most widespread and abundant Jamides in the Malay Peninsula; gardens, secondary growth, and open forest below 1,200m. HW underside with a single subbasal pair of continuous white lines. FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Male: whitish blue upperside with conspicuously broad black FW border reaching at least 1.0mm at the apex. Female: characteristically dingy whitish blue (less clear and bright than J. pura or J. zebra). Tailed at vein 2.

r_pura.name: Jamides pura pura
r_pura.note: Celeno subgroup. Lowland forest; Langkawi Islands to Singapore. HW underside with a single subbasal pair of continuous white lines. FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Underside markings often visible from the upperside by transparency (in males). Male: clear unicolorous blue with thread-like FW border. Female: pale clear blue, brighter and clearer than J. celeno. Tailed at vein 2.

r_zebra.name: Jamides zebra lakatti
r_zebra.note: Celeno subgroup. Primary forest. HW underside with a single subbasal pair of continuous white lines. FW postdiscal band continuous (vein 3–vein 7). Underside markings barely visible from the upperside by transparency. Male: clearer blue than J. celeno; blue deepens toward FW apex; black border slightly and diffusely expanding at the apex. Female: brighter and clearer blue than the dingy J. celeno. Tailed at vein 2.

g_aratus_philatus.group_name: J. aratus / J. philatus (location needed to separate)
g_aratus_philatus.description: Both J. aratus adana and J. philatus subditus have identical HW underside patterns (prominent black wedge-shaped (∧) submarginal spots between the inner and outer submarginal striae). Without knowing where the photo was taken, these species cannot be reliably separated. J. aratus is found on Pulau Tioman and other east-coast islands of Peninsular Malaysia (never on the mainland or in Borneo); J. philatus is a forest species of mainland Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, and Sabah (Borneo). If the upperside is visible, J. aratus is almost white (both sexes); J. philatus male is sky blue with a thread-like FW border. Both are celeno-group species with continuous FW band and white straight striae.
key_features: Prominent black wedge-shaped (∧) spots on HW submarginal area (underside)
Straight white striae (celeno group)
FW postdiscal band continuous, not dislocated at vein 3
g_aratus_philatus.no_next_note: To confirm to species: Pulau Tioman / east-coast islands of Peninsular Malaysia = J. aratus; mainland Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, or Sabah (Borneo) = J. philatus. If location is unknown, the upperside colour can help: almost white = J. aratus; blue = J. philatus.

g_cunilda_limes.group_name: J. cunilda / J. limes (striae definition needed)
g_cunilda_limes.description: Both J. cunilda nisanca and J. limes have FW band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6, costal dashes on the FW, and a large orange tornal area on the HW with continuous subbasal striae. When striae definition cannot be assessed, these species cannot be separated with confidence. J. cunilda occurs on the Malay Peninsula and nearby islands; J. limes is Bornean. J. limes has broad, diffuse striae and is larger (FW ≥18mm); J. cunilda has narrow, clearly defined striae.
key_features: FW postdiscal band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6 (elpis subgroup)
Distinct white costal dashes on FW above end-cell bar
HW subbasal striae continuous (not dislocated)
Large orange tornal area on HW
g_cunilda_limes.no_next_note: To confirm to species, striae definition and size should be assessed: broad/diffuse + large = J. limes (Bornean); narrow/defined = J. cunilda.

g_elpis_caeruleus_ferrari.group_name: J. elpis / J. caeruleus / J. ferrari (HW stria 7 position unclear)
g_elpis_caeruleus_ferrari.description: When the position of the 2nd HW stria in space 7 cannot be determined, these three elpis-subgroup species with narrow clearly-defined striae and no costal dashes cannot be separated. J. elpis has this stria midway between the 2nd and 3rd cell striae; J. caeruleus and J. ferrari have it closer to the 3rd cell stria. See individual species notes for upperside characters — J. caeruleus is the most common of the three.
key_features: FW postdiscal band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6 (elpis subgroup)
No costal dashes on FW above end-cell bar
Narrow, clearly defined white striae
g_elpis_caeruleus_ferrari.no_next_note: To confirm to species, the position of the 2nd HW stria in space 7 must be assessed, or upperside characters used.

g_caeruleus_ferrari.group_name: J. caeruleus / J. ferrari (female spot shape or upperside needed)
g_caeruleus_ferrari.description: Without a clear view of the female lowermost HW submarginal spot shape, J. caeruleus caeruleus and J. ferrari evansi cannot be reliably separated on the underside alone. Both have the 2nd HW stria in space 7 in line with or closer to the 3rd cell stria. J. ferrari is much rarer than J. caeruleus. In males, J. caeruleus is brilliant metallic caerulean blue and lacks battledore androconia; J. ferrari is paler blue with battledore androconia present.
key_features: FW postdiscal band dislocated at vein 3 and vein 6
No costal dashes on FW above end-cell bar
2nd HW stria in space 7 in line with or closer to 3rd cell stria (outer position)
g_caeruleus_ferrari.no_next_note: To confirm to species, check the female lowermost HW submarginal spot (linear = J. ferrari; rounded = J. caeruleus), or assess male upperside colour and androconia.

g_malaccanus_parasaturatus.group_name: J. malaccanus / J. parasaturatus (transparency check needed)
g_malaccanus_parasaturatus.description: Both J. malaccanus malaccanus and J. parasaturatus paramalaccanus have two pairs of continuous white lines on the HW underside. Without assessing transparency (visibility of underside markings from the upperside), these two primary-forest species cannot be separated. J. malaccanus has deeper sky blue upperside and distinct HW submarginal spots on the upperside; J. parasaturatus is paler blue and lacks HW submarginal markings.
key_features: Two pairs of continuous white lines on HW underside (subbasal + discal)
FW postdiscal band continuous, not dislocated at vein 3 (celeno subgroup)
Straight white striae
g_malaccanus_parasaturatus.no_next_note: To confirm to species, hold the specimen or photo up to the light or assess the upperside: markings clearly visible = J. parasaturatus; barely visible = J. malaccanus.

g_celeno_pura_zebra.group_name: J. celeno / J. pura / J. zebra (FW border width needed)
g_celeno_pura_zebra.description: Without assessing the male forewing black border width, these three celeno-subgroup species with a single subbasal pair of HW white lines cannot be distinguished. J. celeno has a conspicuously broad FW border (at least 1.0mm at apex); J. pura has a thread-like border; J. zebra has a slightly and diffusely expanding border. Females: J. celeno is dingy whitish blue; J. pura and J. zebra are brighter and clearer blue.
key_features: Single subbasal pair of continuous white lines on HW underside
FW postdiscal band continuous, not dislocated at vein 3 (celeno subgroup)
g_celeno_pura_zebra.no_next_note: To confirm to species, assess the male FW border width from the upperside: broad ≥1mm = J. celeno; narrow/thread = J. pura or J. zebra (check transparency to separate those two).

g_pura_zebra.group_name: J. pura / J. zebra (transparency check needed)
g_pura_zebra.description: Without assessing transparency (visibility of underside markings from the upperside), J. pura pura and J. zebra lakatti cannot be separated on the underside alone. Both have a single subbasal pair of HW white lines and a narrow or thread-like FW border. In J. pura, the underside markings are often visible through the wing; in J. zebra, they are barely visible. Both species prefer primary forest.
key_features: Single subbasal pair of white lines on HW underside
FW postdiscal band continuous (celeno subgroup)
Narrow to thread-like FW black border (male)
g_pura_zebra.no_next_note: To confirm to species, hold the specimen up to the light: markings visible = J. pura; barely visible = J. zebra.
